---
title: "Female Representation in Management of Australian Organisations" 
subtitle: "A Multi-Class Classification Study Using the 2024 WGEA Dataset"
author: "W13_G01 (SID: 510546500, 4803773225, 500180477, 530729668, 540321609)"
output:
  bookdown::html_document2:
    code_folding: hide
    self_contained: true
  bookdown::pdf_document2:
    latex_engine: xelatex
fontsize: 10pt
geometry: margin=1in
editor_options:
  markdown:
    wrap: 72
---

```{r}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(fig.align = "center")
```

![](IntroPicture.png){width=70% style="display:block; margin-left:auto; margin-right:auto;"}


## Problem Definition

Achieving gender equity in the workplace is a critical national goal for
Australia, yet employers often face unique structural and cultural
barriers that hinder women’s progression into leadership roles. This
project therefore investigates the following research question:

**“Can workforce composition, promotion and resignation patterns, and
organisational gender-equity policies be used to predict the level of
female representation in management positions within Australian
organisations?”**

This study is presented as a **classification problem**, where each
organisation is assigned to one of **five quintile-based categories**
based on the percentage of managers who are female: *Very Low, Low,
Moderate, High, and Very High* female representation in management.

The practical importance of this work lies in the established link
between gender diversity in leadership and improved organisational
performance. Research by the Workplace Gender Equality Agency and the
Bankwest Curtin Economics Centre found that a **10-percentage-point
increase in female top-tier managers corresponded to a 6.6% uplift in
the market value** of ASX-listed companies
[[1]](https://www.wgea.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/BCEC%20WGEA%20Media%20Release%20-%20More%20women%20at%20the%20top%20proves%20better%20for%20business.pdf).
By identifying the organisational factors that predict high female
management representation, this analysis can inform evidence-based
interventions that support women’s advancement into management roles,
delivering benefits for both workplace equity and business outcomes.

## Data Description

### Data Overview

The data for this investigation are drawn from the **Workplace Gender
Equality Agency (WGEA) Public Dataset 2024**
[[2]](https://data.gov.au/data/dataset/wgea-dataset), which comprises
mandated survey responses from 7415 Australian employers with 100 or
more employees. The dataset is provided across seven files, including:

**Quantitative Workforce Data (2 files):** These files provide
disaggregated counts of employees and workforce movements for each
employer in long format. Each row represents a specific subgroup within
a company, defined by attributes such as role, employment type, and
gender (e.g., CEO – Full-time – Female).

-   **Workforce Composition (193,916 rows; 17 columns):** Contains
    employee counts by occupation, management category, employment type,
    and gender, showing a breakdown of workforce structure across
    employers.

-   **Workforce Management Statistics (186,831 rows; 18 columns):**
    Reports workforce movements (e.g., promotions, resignations) by
    gender, employment status, and managerial category.

**Questionnaire Data (5 files):** These files capture organisational
policies and practices in long format, where each row corresponds to a
single employer’s response to one question. For questions allowing
multiple selections, each chosen option is recorded as a separate row
(e.g., an employer selecting three flexible-work options generates three
rows). Collectively, the five files contain 83 unique questions and 554
unique responses, covering: Harm Prevention (508,476 rows), Action on
Gender Equality (129,313 rows), Flexible Work (222,858 rows), Workplace
Overview (178,111 rows), Employee Support (289,830 rows).

### Challenges

The chosen dataset presents several challenges:

-   **High Dimensionality:** The feature space is large due to binary
    encoding of questionnaire data. Across 83 unique questions and 554
    response options, the resulting dataset combines quantitative
    workforce metrics with hundreds of binary policy indicators,
    creating a high dimensional input space.

-   **Integration Complexity:** Source files are provided in long format
    and at differing levels of granularity, requiring feature
    engineering and aggregation to produce a wide-format dataset
    suitable for modelling.

-   **Data Quality and Missingness:** Missing values are common due to
    inconsistent reporting and the non-applicability of certain
    questions across employers, necessitating imputation.

## Data Preparation and Feature Engineering

The raw WGEA data were processed to produce a dataset suitable for
modelling. Each employer was represented by a single row containing
quantitative metrics and binary indicators derived from the source
files.

**1. Data Integration** The seven datasets were merged on the shared
employer_abn key, converting the original long format structure into
aggregated employer-level feature sets.

**2. Feature Engineering** New features were engineered to create a
selected set of predictors for the classification task:

-   **Workforce Composition Features:** both the overall and the
    gender-specific proportions of management, executive, full-time, and
    part-time roles within each organisation (e.g. `percent_full_time`
    and `full_time_female_percent`).

-   **Workforce Movement Features:** gender-specific promotion and
    resignation rates to highlight potential disparities in workforce
    movement.

-   **Organisational Policy Features:** Binary indicators reflecting the
    presence of gender-equity, flexible-work, and harm-prevention
    policies.

**3. Target Variable Creation** The categorical target
*management_representation_level* was derived from the engineered
variable *management_female_percent* (the percentage of managers who are
women) by dividing organisations into five balanced quintiles: *Very
Low, Low, Moderate, High, and Very High*. Organisations with no
management employees were excluded, as representation could not be
determined.

This approach:

-   Ensures balanced class sizes, with each category containing \~20 %
    of organisations; and

-   Captures the full spectrum of management gender representation while
    maintaining interpretability.

**4. Handling of Missing Values** Missing values were handled using the
following multi-step process:

-   **Removal of Unlinkable Records:** Any rows missing an
    `employer_abn` were discarded. These records often belonged to
    subsidiaries of larger corporate groups that had workforce data but
    lacked their own corresponding questionnaire data.

-   **Imputation for Numeric and Binary Features:** For all remaining
    numeric and binary policy features, missing values (`NA`) were
    imputed with zero. This assumes that an absence of reported data
    signifies a true zero value, since organisations are not only
    incentivised to report their positive policies, but also mandated to
    report its statistics.

-   **Imputation for Categorical Features:** For categorical features
    where a zero was not a logical substitute (e.g.,
    `top_leader_gender`), missing values were replaced with a new
    category such as '"None Reported"'. This allows us to assess if the
    absence of a response is a predictive pattern.

After the feature engineering and filtering out organisations with no
management positions, the final dataset comprised **6,673 employer-level
records** and **32 features** suitable for identifying patterns of
gender representation in management.

```{r, echo=TRUE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
### Import libraries
library(tidyverse)
library(here)
library(dplyr)
library(naniar)
library(FactoMineR)
library(factoextra)
library(visdat)
library(patchwork)
library(ggplot2)
library(Rtsne)
library(gridExtra)
library(psych)
library(reshape2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(ggridges)
library(corrplot)
library(ggcorrplot)


### Load the data
df_comp <- read_csv("wgea_workforce_composition_2024.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  drop_na(employer_abn)

df_mgmt <- read_csv("wgea_workforce_management_statistics_2024.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  drop_na(employer_abn)

df_actions <- read_csv("wgea_questionnaire_action_on_gender_equality_2024.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  drop_na(employer_abn)

df_flex <- read_csv("wgea_questionnaire_flexible_work_2024.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  drop_na(employer_abn)

df_overview <- read_csv("wgea_questionnaire_workplace_overview_2024.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  drop_na(employer_abn)

df_harm <- read_csv("wgea_questionnaire_harm_prevention_2024.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  drop_na(employer_abn)

df_support <- read_csv("wgea_questionnaire_employee_support_2024.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  drop_na(employer_abn)


### Feature engineering: workforce composition data
create_composition_features <- function(df_comp) {
  # --- Helper columns for aggregation ---
  df_comp <- df_comp %>%
    mutate(
      is_manager   = as.integer(manager_category == 'Manager'),
      is_executive = as.integer(occupation %in% c(
        'CEOs', 'Heads of Business', 'Other Executives and General Managers',
        'Key Management Personnel', 'Senior managers')),
      is_full_time = as.integer(employment_status == 'Full-time'),
      is_part_time = as.integer(employment_status == 'Part-time'),
      is_casual    = as.integer(employment_status == 'Casual'),
      is_female    = as.integer(gender == 'Women')
    )

  # --- Aggregation by employer ABN ---
  features <- df_comp %>%
    group_by(employer_abn) %>%
    summarise(
      total_employees    = sum(n_employees),  # keep NA if all missing
      percent_managers   = sum(n_employees * is_manager)   / total_employees,
      percent_full_time  = sum(n_employees * is_full_time) / total_employees,
      percent_part_time  = sum(n_employees * is_part_time) / total_employees,
      percent_casual     = sum(n_employees * is_casual)    / total_employees,
      percent_female     = sum(n_employees * is_female)    / total_employees,
      .groups = 'drop'
    )

  # --- Top Leader Gender ---
  top_leader_df <- df_comp %>%
  filter(occupation == "CEOs") %>%
  group_by(employer_abn) %>%
  summarise(
    top_leader_gender = case_when(
      n_distinct(gender) == 0 ~ "Non_specified",
      n_distinct(gender) == 1 ~ first(gender),
      n_distinct(gender) > 1  ~ "Both/Mix"
    ),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

  features <- features %>%
  left_join(top_leader_df, by = "employer_abn") %>%
  mutate(
    top_leader_gender = ifelse(
      is.na(top_leader_gender), "Non_specified", top_leader_gender
    ),
    top_leader_gender = factor(
      top_leader_gender,
      levels = c("Men", "Women", "Both/Mix", "Non_specified")
    )
  )
  # --- Gender Composition by Role/Status ---
  calculate_female_percent <- function(df, filter_expr) {
    df %>%
      filter({{filter_expr}}) %>%
      group_by(employer_abn, gender) %>%
      summarise(n = sum(n_employees), .groups = 'drop') %>%
      pivot_wider(names_from = gender, values_from = n) %>%   # don't fill NAs
      mutate(female_percent = ifelse((Women + Men) > 0, Women / (Women + Men), NA_real_)) %>%
      select(employer_abn, female_percent)
  }

  manager_gender     <- calculate_female_percent(df_comp, is_manager == 1)
  executive_gender   <- calculate_female_percent(df_comp, is_executive == 1)
  non_manager_gender <- calculate_female_percent(df_comp, is_manager == 0)
  full_time_gender   <- calculate_female_percent(df_comp, is_full_time == 1)
  part_time_gender   <- calculate_female_percent(df_comp, is_part_time == 1)
  casual_gender      <- calculate_female_percent(df_comp, is_casual == 1)

  features <- features %>%
    left_join(manager_gender   %>% rename(management_female_percent      = female_percent), by = "employer_abn") %>%
    left_join(executive_gender %>% rename(executive_roles_female_percent = female_percent), by = "employer_abn") %>%
    left_join(non_manager_gender %>% rename(non_management_female_percent = female_percent), by = "employer_abn") %>%
    left_join(full_time_gender   %>% rename(full_time_female_percent      = female_percent), by = "employer_abn") %>%
    left_join(part_time_gender   %>% rename(part_time_female_percent      = female_percent), by = "employer_abn") %>%
    left_join(casual_gender      %>% rename(casual_female_percent         = female_percent), by = "employer_abn")

  return(features)
}


### Feature engineering: questionnaire data

# Engineers binary features from the questionnaire data
create_policy_features <- function(df_actions, df_flex, df_overview, df_harm, df_support, abn_list) {
  # --- Setup and create helper functions ---
  df_q <- bind_rows(df_actions, df_flex, df_overview, df_harm, df_support) %>%
    distinct()

  policy_features <- tibble(employer_abn = unique(abn_list))

  add_binary_feature <- function(df, feature_name, question_text, response_value) {
    abns_with_policy <- df_q %>%
      filter(str_starts(question_text, !!question_text), response == !!response_value) %>%
      pull(employer_abn) %>%
      unique()

    df %>% mutate(!!feature_name := as.integer(employer_abn %in% abns_with_policy))
  }

  # --- Core Strategy & Pay Equity ---
  policy_features <- policy_features %>%
    add_binary_feature('has_gender_equality_strategy', "1.1", "Yes") %>%
    add_binary_feature('has_target_for_gender_equity', "1.2", "Yes") %>%
    add_binary_feature('has_policy_for_gender_equality', "1.4e", "Yes") %>%
    add_binary_feature('has_target_for_women_in_governing_body', "1.4g", "Yes") %>%
    add_binary_feature('has_pay_equity_strategy', "2.1", "Yes") %>%
    add_binary_feature('did_pay_gap_analysis', "2.2", "Yes") %>%
    add_binary_feature('actioned_on_pay_gap_analysis', "2.2c", "Yes") %>%
    add_binary_feature('consulted_employee_on_equity', "2.4", "Yes")

  # --- Flexible Work ---
  policy_features <- policy_features %>%
    add_binary_feature('has_flexible_work_policy', "3.1", "Yes") 

  # --- Parental Leave ---
  policy_features <- policy_features %>%
    add_binary_feature('offers_paid_primary_carer_leave', "4.1.2", "Yes") %>%
    add_binary_feature('offers_paid_secondary_carer_leave', "4.1.3", "Yes") %>%
    add_binary_feature('opt_out_approach_for_parental_leave', "4.2", "Yes") %>%
    add_binary_feature('has_strategy_for_employee_with_family', "4.4", "Yes") %>%
    add_binary_feature('has_target_for_men_parental_leave', "1.2", "Increase the number of men taking parental leave")

  # --- Employee Support & Training ---
  abns_manager_training <- df_q %>%
    filter(str_detect(question_text, "Did you take any actions as a result of your gender remuneration gap analysis?"),
           response == "Trained people-managers in addressing gender bias (including unconscious bias)") %>%
    pull(employer_abn) %>%
    unique()
  policy_features <- policy_features %>%
    mutate(gender_equality_training_for_managers = as.integer(employer_abn %in% abns_manager_training))

  return(policy_features)
}

### Feature engineering: workforce movement
create_movement_features <- function(df_movement, df_composition) {
  if (is.null(df_movement) || is.null(df_composition)) return(NULL)

  # 1. Get total employees by gender and rename for clarity
  comp_gender_totals <- df_composition %>%
    group_by(employer_abn, gender) %>%
    summarise(total = sum(n_employees), .groups = 'drop') %>%
    pivot_wider(names_from = gender, values_from = total) %>%
    rename(Men_total = Men, Women_total = Women)

  # 2. Get all movement counts by type
  movements_by_type <- df_movement %>%
    group_by(employer_abn, movement_type, gender) %>%
    summarise(n = sum(n_employees), .groups = 'drop') %>%
    pivot_wider(names_from = gender, values_from = n)

  # 3. Filter for promotions and rename
  promotions <- movements_by_type %>%
    filter(movement_type == 'Promotions') %>%
    select(employer_abn, Men, Women) %>%
    rename(Men_promo = Men, Women_promo = Women)

  # 4. Filter for resignations and rename
  resignations <- movements_by_type %>%
    filter(movement_type == 'Resignations') %>%
    select(employer_abn, Men, Women) %>%
    rename(Men_resign = Men, Women_resign = Women)

  # 5. Join and calculate rates (keep NA if totals or counts missing)
rates <- comp_gender_totals %>%
  left_join(promotions, by = "employer_abn") %>%
  left_join(resignations, by = "employer_abn") %>%
  mutate(
    promotion_rate_female = ifelse(
      !is.na(Women_total) & (Women_total + Women_promo) > 0,
      Women_promo / (Women_total + Women_promo),
      NA_real_
    ),
    promotion_rate_male = ifelse(
      !is.na(Men_total) & (Men_total + Men_promo) > 0,
      Men_promo / (Men_total + Men_promo),
      NA_real_
    ),
    resignation_rate_female = ifelse(
      !is.na(Women_total) & (Women_total + Women_resign) > 0,
      Women_resign / (Women_total + Women_resign),
      NA_real_
    ),
    resignation_rate_male = ifelse(
      !is.na(Men_total) & (Men_total + Men_resign) > 0,
      Men_resign / (Men_total + Men_resign),
      NA_real_
    )
  )

  # 6. Combine all movement features
  final_movement_features <- rates %>%
    select(
      employer_abn,
      promotion_rate_female, promotion_rate_male,
      resignation_rate_female, resignation_rate_male
    )

  return(final_movement_features)
}

### Main Execution
composition_features <- create_composition_features(df_comp)
policy_features <- create_policy_features(df_actions, df_flex, df_overview, df_harm, df_support, composition_features$employer_abn)
movement_features <- create_movement_features(df_mgmt, df_comp)

# Join all features together
composite_df <- composition_features %>%
  left_join(policy_features, by = "employer_abn") %>%
  left_join(movement_features, by = "employer_abn")

# Add industry info
industry_info <- df_comp %>%
  select(employer_abn, anzsic_division) %>%
  distinct()

composite_df <- composite_df %>%
  left_join(industry_info, by = "employer_abn")


### Drop organizations with no management data
# Remove organizations where management_female_percent is NA (no managers at all)
composite_df <- composite_df %>%
  filter(!is.na(management_female_percent))

### Handle remaining missing values
composite_df <- composite_df %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~replace_na(., 0))) %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.character), ~replace_na(., "None")))

### Create target variable: management representation level buckets
# Create balanced quintiles using ntile() to ensure exactly 20% in each group
composite_df <- composite_df %>%
  mutate(
    quintile_rank = ntile(management_female_percent, 5),
    management_representation_level = case_when(
      quintile_rank == 1 ~ "V.Low",
      quintile_rank == 2 ~ "Low", 
      quintile_rank == 3 ~ "Mod",
      quintile_rank == 4 ~ "High",
      quintile_rank == 5 ~ "V.High"
    )
  ) %>%
  select(-quintile_rank) %>% 
  mutate(
  management_representation_level = factor(
    management_representation_level,
    levels = c("V.Low", "Low", "Mod", "High", "V.High")
  )
) %>% select(-percent_casual, -percent_female)
  
```

## Exploratory Analysis and Visualisation

### Distribution of Female Representation in Management

```{r fig-target, fig.cap="Target variable histogram", fig.width=7, fig.height=3, warning=FALSE, error=FALSE, message=FALSE}
# Compute mean and median
mean_val <- mean(composite_df$management_female_percent, na.rm = TRUE)
median_val <- median(composite_df$management_female_percent, na.rm = TRUE)

# Plot histogram with mean and median lines
ggplot(composite_df, aes(x = management_female_percent, fill = management_representation_level)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, alpha = 0.7, color = "white", size = 0.3) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = mean_val), color = "blue", linetype = "dashed", size = 1) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = median_val), color = "red", linetype = "dotted", size = 1) +
  annotate("text", x = mean_val + 0.03, y = 380, label = "Mean = 0.416", color = "blue", hjust = 0, size = 3.5) +
  annotate("text", x = median_val + 0.03, y = 350, label = "Median = 0.400", color = "red", hjust = 0, size = 3.5) +
  labs(
    x = "Management Female Percentage",
    y = "Count",
    fill = "Female\nManagement\nRepresentation\nLevel",
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "right",
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold")
  ) +
  guides(fill = guide_legend(ncol = 1))
```

The distribution of female management representation across
organisations is distinctly right-skewed, with the majority of employers
reporting relatively low proportions of women in management roles. The
mean female management share is 41.6%, and the median is 40%, confirming
that most organisations fall below gender parity. The histogram shows
that a large concentration of firms lie between 20% and 40%
representation, while only a minority of firms exceed equal
representation. This indicates that women remain underrepresented in
managerial positions, and gender balance at management levels is still
relatively uncommon across Australian organisations. The skewed pattern
highlights that progress toward equality is uneven and concentrated
among a small subset of firms.

### Boxplots of Non-Binary Numeric Features (with Outlier Counts)

```{r fig-boxplot, fig.cap="Boxplots of non-binary numeric variables", fig.width=8, fig.height=3}
# 1. Select numeric (non-binary) features
df_num <- composite_df %>%
  select(where(is.numeric), -employer_abn, -total_employees, -management_female_percent) %>%
  select(where(~ n_distinct(.) > 2))

# 2. Reshape to long format for plotting
df_num_long <- df_num %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Feature", values_to = "Value")

# 3. Compute outlier counts per feature
outlier_counts <- df_num_long %>%
  group_by(Feature) %>%
  mutate(
    Q1 = quantile(Value, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
    Q3 = quantile(Value, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    IQR = Q3 - Q1,
    lower = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR,
    upper = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR,
    is_outlier = Value < lower | Value > upper
  ) %>%
  summarise(outlier_count = sum(is_outlier, na.rm = TRUE))

# 4. Global max across all features for fixed label position
global_max <- max(df_num_long$Value, na.rm = TRUE)

# 5. Plot boxplots with right-aligned labels
ggplot(df_num_long, aes(x = Feature, y = Value)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "lightblue", alpha = 0.7,
               outlier.colour = "red", outlier.size = 1) +
  coord_flip(clip = "off") +
  geom_text(
    data = outlier_counts,
    aes(x = Feature,
        y = global_max * 1.05,
        label = paste0("Outliers: ", outlier_count)),
    inherit.aes = FALSE, hjust = 0, size = 3.2
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Feature",
    y = "Value"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.margin = margin(5, 110, 5, 5))
```

The boxplots illustrate the distribution of non-binary numeric features,
with outlier counts shown for each variable. High outlier frequencies
are observed in promotion and resignation rates, as well as the
proportion of part-time and casual employees, whereas gender composition
percentages are generally well-bounded with few or no outliers. This
indicates that variability in workforce dynamics is concentrated in
employment status and movement measures rather than overall gender
proportions.

Although several variables show a high number of outliers, further
inspection of the raw WGEA data confirmed that these values are genuine
and reflect real organisational differences rather than data errors. The
dataset includes employers of varying sizes, industries, and workforce
structures, meaning that naturally some organisations will have much
higher or lower promotion, resignation, or part-time rates than others.
These outliers therefore represent meaningful variation rather than
noise, and were retained in the analysis to preserve the true diversity
of organisational characteristics across the sample.


### PCA of Non-binary Numeric Features

```{r fig-pca, fig.cap = "PCA and Scree plot", fig.width=15, fig.height=7}
library(ggrepel)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(patchwork)

# ============================================================
# 1. PCA Calculation
# ============================================================
df_scaled <- scale(df_num)
pca_res <- prcomp(df_scaled, center = FALSE, scale. = FALSE)

pca_df <- data.frame(
  PC1 = pca_res$x[, 1],
  PC2 = pca_res$x[, 2],
  Female_Mgmt_Level = composite_df$management_representation_level
)

# ============================================================
# 2. Select Top Loadings (most influential features)
# ============================================================
loadings <- as.data.frame(pca_res$rotation[, 1:2])
loadings$Variable <- rownames(loadings)

top_loadings <- loadings %>%
  mutate(Magnitude = sqrt(PC1^2 + PC2^2)) %>%
  slice_max(order_by = Magnitude, n = 5)

arrow_scale <- 4
top_loadings <- top_loadings %>%
  mutate(PC1_arrow = PC1 * arrow_scale,
         PC2_arrow = PC2 * arrow_scale)

# ============================================================
# 3. PCA scatterplot with loadings (larger text)
# ============================================================
p1 <- ggplot(pca_df, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = Female_Mgmt_Level)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 2.5) +
  geom_segment(
    data = top_loadings,
    aes(x = 0, y = 0, xend = PC1_arrow, yend = PC2_arrow),
    arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm")),
    color = "black",
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data = top_loadings,
    aes(x = PC1_arrow * 1.1, y = PC2_arrow * 1.1, label = Variable),
    size = 4.5,              # <- enlarged feature labels
    color = "black",
    box.padding = 0.5,
    min.segment.length = 0,
    max.overlaps = Inf
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "PCA with Top Feature Loadings",
    x = "Principal Component 1",
    y = "Principal Component 2",
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 15) +       # <- larger base font for all text
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold", size = 16),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 12),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 12)
  ) +
  scale_color_discrete(
    name = "Female Management Representation Level",
    labels = function(x) str_wrap(x, width = 12)
  )

# ============================================================
# 4. Scree Plot (optional beside)
# ============================================================
pca_var_exp <- (pca_res$sdev^2) / sum(pca_res$sdev^2)
cum_var_exp <- cumsum(pca_var_exp) * 100

p2 <- ggplot(data.frame(
  Component = seq_along(cum_var_exp),
  CumulativeVariance = cum_var_exp
), aes(x = Component, y = CumulativeVariance)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue", size = 3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 80, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  labs(
    title = "Cumulative Scree Plot",
    x = "Principal Component",
    y = "Cumulative Var. Explained (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 15) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold", size = 16),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 12)
  )

# ============================================================
# 5. Combine
# ============================================================
(p1 | p2) + plot_layout(widths = c(2, 1))

```

The PCA offers a parametric, global, and linear perspective on workforce
composition and management representation, with the first two principal
components capturing approximately 53.7% of total variance (PC1 = 35.3%,
PC2 = 18.4%). The scatterplot reveals considerable overlap among female
management levels, though a slight separation trend along PC1 suggests
that organisational differences in workforce structure partially align
with management gender balance. The scree plot confirms that explanatory
power diminishes sharply after the first three components, indicating
that most meaningful variation is captured in the initial dimensions.
Feature loadings demonstrate that organisational differences are
primarily driven by promotion and resignation rates across both genders,
reflecting common patterns in career mobility and turnover, while
non-management female percentage contributes more distinctly along PC1
axis.

### t-SNE Projection of Non-binary Numeric Features

```{r fig-tsne, fig.cap="tSNE Projection", fig.width=6.5, fig.height=4}
set.seed(5003)
df_unique <- unique(df_scaled)
mgmt_unique <- composite_df$management_representation_level[!duplicated(df_scaled)]
tsne_res <- Rtsne(df_unique, dims = 2, perplexity = 30, verbose = FALSE)

tsne_df <- data.frame(
  Dim1 = tsne_res$Y[,1],
  Dim2 = tsne_res$Y[,2],
  Management_Level = mgmt_unique
)

ggplot(tsne_df, aes(Dim1, Dim2, color = Management_Level)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 1.2) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.box = "right",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 6)
  ) +
  guides(color = guide_legend(nrow = 5, byrow = TRUE)) +
  scale_color_discrete(
    name = "Female\nManagement\nRepresentation\nLevel",
    labels = function(x) str_wrap(x, width = 12)
  )
```

The t-SNE projection provides a non-parametric, local, and non-linear
view of the dataset that complements PCA's global linear perspective by
preserving local relationships between observations and revealing more
nuanced data structure. The visualisation demonstrates that
organisations are broadly grouped by female management representation
levels, with Very Low (red) and Very High (purple) categories forming
partial clusters at opposite ends of the embedding, while intermediate
groups (Low, Moderate, High) exhibit greater overlap, suggesting gradual
structural transitions rather than sharp boundaries. These patterns
indicate that relationships between organisational features and
management representation are non-linear in nature, driven by complex
feature interactions that create subtle separations. The clearer
clustering observed in t-SNE compared to PCA suggests that non-linear
models such as XGBoost and Decision Trees may be better suited to
capture these intricate patterns and potentially improve predictive
performance over purely linear approaches.

## Modelling Plan

A combination of linear, distance-based, tree-based, kernel-based, and
ensemble algorithms was implemented to compare interpretability and
predictive accuracy across models of increasing complexity. All models
were trained within the **caret** framework using identical **five-fold
stratified cross-validation**, ensuring fair and directly comparable
evaluation. Predictor preprocessing steps (centering, scaling, and
removal of zero-variance features) were applied consistently across all
models.

### Model Selection and Hyperparameter Tuning

```{r}
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)

model_table <- tribble(
  ~Model, ~Rationale, ~Tuning_Method, ~Hyperparameters_Search, ~Final_Parameters,
  
  # Multinomial Logistic Regression
  "Multinomial Logistic Regression (MLR)",
  "Serves as a transparent linear baseline to quantify directional relationships between organisational features and management representation levels.",
  "Default optimisation",
  "No explicit tuning. *Ridge and Lasso regression were tested but excluded for conciseness due to minimal performance difference.*",
  "N/A",
  
  # KNN
  "K-Nearest Neighbours (KNN)",
  "Captures non-linear, similarity-based relationships in the data space, complementing linear models.",
  "Manual grid search",
  "`k ∈ {3–50}` (48 evenly spaced values)",
  "`k = 31`",
  
  # Decision Tree
  "Decision Tree",
  "Provides interpretable, rule-based splits and captures threshold effects in quantitative features.",
  "Manual grid search",
  "`cp ∈ [0.001, 0.1]` (1,000 evenly spaced values)",
  "`cp = 0.0016`",
  
  # SVM
  "Support Vector Machine (RBF Kernel)",
  "Captures complex non-linear decision boundaries using kernel transformations, suitable for high-dimensional spaces.",
  "Automatic search* (`tuneLength = 20`)",
  "20 automatic combinations across `C` and `sigma`.",
  "`C = 4`, `sigma = 0.0126`",
  
  # XGBoost
  "XGBoost",
  "High-performance ensemble model that captures complex feature interactions while maintaining robustness to overfitting.",
  "Automatic search* (`tuneLength = 20`)",
  "20 automatic combinations across `nrounds`, `max_depth`, `eta`, `gamma`, `colsample_bytree`, `min_child_weight`, and `subsample`.",
  "`nrounds = 978`, `max_depth = 3`, `eta = 0.0952`, `gamma = 2.0184`, `colsample_bytree = 0.464`, `min_child_weight = 14`, `subsample = 0.995`"
)

model_table %>%
  kbl(
    align = "lllll",
    escape = FALSE,
    caption = "Model Overview, Tuning Approach, and Optimal Hyperparameters",
    col.names = c(
    "Model",
    "Rationale",
    "Tuning Method",
    "Hyperparameters & Search Range",
    "Final Cross-Validated Parameters"
  )
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(
    full_width = FALSE,
    bootstrap_options = c("hover", "condensed"),
    font_size = 12
  ) %>%
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE) %>%
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE, width = "3cm") %>%
  column_spec(2, width = "6cm") %>%
  column_spec(3:5, width = "3cm")

```


All tuning procedures were conducted using **identical cross-validation
folds** to ensure methodological consistency. None of the tuned
parameters reached boundary values, confirming that the search space was
sufficiently broad to capture near-optimal configurations.

\**Note: SVM and XGBoost models were tuned using automatic
hyperparameter search to reduce computation time, as even moderately
spaced grid search required several hours (\>6 hours).*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Model Training Pipeline

All models were trained and evaluated within a unified pipeline to
ensure methodological consistency:

1.  **Cross-Validation:** Stratified five-fold CV preserving class
    balance across folds, with identical splits applied to all models.\
2.  **Preprocessing:** Centering, scaling, and zero-variance feature
    removal performed within each fold.\
3.  **Probability Estimation:** Class probabilities extracted (where
    supported) for AUC evaluation.

This consistent framework ensures that observed performance differences
reflect genuine algorithmic variation rather than preprocessing or
sampling artefacts.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Evaluation Strategy

Model performance is assessed using the **F1-score as the primary
ranking metric**. As all five management representation categories are
evenly distributed, it provides an unbiased measure of predictive
performance across classes. 

The following complementary metrics are also reported:

-   **Precision:** The proportion of correct predictions within each
    class, reflecting the model's ability to minimise false positives.

-   **Recall:** The proportion of actual cases correctly identified,
    reflecting the model's ability to minimise false negatives.

-   **AUC:** Evaluates the model’s ability to distinguish each class
    against all others across all decision thresholds. Higher values
    indicate stronger class separability.

-   **Mean Absolute Error (MAE):** The average ordinal distance between
    predicted and actual classes, penalising predictions proportionally
    to how far they deviate from the true class.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Results

```{r fig.height=6, fig.width=15, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Load saved variables to reduce knitting time between non-code edits (>6 hour model training time)
setwd("~/Work/Masters Degree/STAT5003/Group Assignment/wgea_public_dataset_2024_v2")
load("ReportData.RData")

# # ============================================================
# # Combined Model Training: Logistic, Ridge, Lasso, Tree, KNN, SVM, XGBoost
# # All models use identical 5-fold cross-validation splits
# # ============================================================
# 
# library(caret)
# library(glmnet)
# library(nnet)
# library(rpart)
# library(kernlab)
# library(xgboost)
# library(dplyr)
# library(ggplot2)
# library(stringr)
# 
# set.seed(5003)
# 
# # -----------------------------
# # Prepare data
# # -----------------------------
# modeling_df <- composite_df %>%
#   select(-employer_abn, -management_female_percent) %>%
#   drop_na()
# 
# # Create consistent folds for all models
# folds <- createFolds(modeling_df$management_representation_level, k = 5, returnTrain = TRUE)
# 
# # Define shared trainControl using the same folds
# shared_control <- trainControl(
#   method = "cv",
#   number = 5,
#   classProbs = TRUE,
#   summaryFunction = multiClassSummary,
#   savePredictions = "final",
#   index = folds
# )
# 
# # ============================================================
# # 1. Multinomial Logistic Regression
# # ============================================================
# multinom_cv <- train(
#   management_representation_level ~ .,
#   data = modeling_df,
#   preProcess = c("zv", "center", "scale"),
#   method = "multinom",
#   trControl = shared_control,
#   trace = FALSE
# )
# 
# 
# # ============================================================
# # 2. Decision Tree
# # ============================================================
# tree_cv <- train(
#   management_representation_level ~ .,
#   data = modeling_df,
#   method = "rpart",
#   trControl = shared_control,
#   preProcess = c("zv", "center", "scale"),
#   tuneGrid = expand.grid(cp = seq(0.0001, 0.1000, length = 1000))
# )
# 
# # ============================================================
# # 3. K-Nearest Neighbours
# # ============================================================
# knn_cv <- train(
#   management_representation_level ~ .,
#   data = modeling_df,
#   method = "knn",
#   trControl = shared_control,
#   preProcess = c("zv", "center", "scale"),
#   tuneGrid = expand.grid(k = seq(3, 50, by = 1))  # manually search from 3 to 50 neighbours
# )
# 
# # ============================================================
# # 4. Support Vector Machine
# # ============================================================
# svm_cv <- train(
#   management_representation_level ~ .,
#   data = modeling_df,
#   method = "svmRadial",
#   trControl = shared_control,
#   preProcess = c("zv", "center", "scale"),
#   tuneLength = 20,
#   metric = "F1",
#   verbose = FALSE
# )
# 
# 
# # ============================================================
# # 5. XGBoost (Optimized Grid Search)
# # ============================================================
# xgb_cv <- train(
#   management_representation_level ~ .,
#   data = modeling_df,
#   method = "xgbTree",
#   trControl = shared_control,
#   preProcess = c("zv", "center", "scale"),
#   tuneLength = 20,
#   metric = "F1",
#   verbose = FALSE
# )
```

### Model Results Summary

```{r message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.width=15, fig.height=6}
library(caret)
library(pROC)
library(dplyr)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)

# ============================================================
# Extract Predictions for Confusion Matrices
# ============================================================
pred_multinom <- multinom_cv$pred
pred_tree     <- tree_cv$pred
pred_knn      <- knn_cv$pred
pred_svm      <- svm_cv$pred
pred_xgb      <- xgb_cv$pred

# ============================================================
# Confusion Matrices + AUCs
# ============================================================
cm_multinom <- confusionMatrix(pred_multinom$pred, pred_multinom$obs)
auc_multinom <- multiclass.roc(pred_multinom$obs, as.numeric(pred_multinom$pred))$auc

cm_tree <- confusionMatrix(pred_tree$pred, pred_tree$obs)
auc_tree <- multiclass.roc(pred_tree$obs, as.numeric(pred_tree$pred))$auc

cm_knn <- confusionMatrix(pred_knn$pred, pred_knn$obs)
auc_knn <- multiclass.roc(pred_knn$obs, as.numeric(pred_knn$pred))$auc

cm_svm <- confusionMatrix(pred_svm$pred, pred_svm$obs)
auc_svm <- multiclass.roc(pred_svm$obs, as.numeric(pred_svm$pred))$auc

cm_xgb <- confusionMatrix(pred_xgb$pred, pred_xgb$obs)
auc_xgb <- multiclass.roc(pred_xgb$obs, as.numeric(pred_xgb$pred))$auc


# ============================================================
# Compute MAE for Ordinal Classes
# ============================================================
compute_mae <- function(obs, pred) {
  obs_num <- as.numeric(factor(obs, levels = levels(obs)))
  pred_num <- as.numeric(factor(pred, levels = levels(obs)))
  mean(abs(obs_num - pred_num))
}

# ============================================================
# Confusion Matrices + AUCs + MAEs
# ============================================================
get_metrics <- function(pred_df) {
  cm <- confusionMatrix(pred_df$pred, pred_df$obs)
  auc <- multiclass.roc(pred_df$obs, as.numeric(pred_df$pred))$auc
  mae <- compute_mae(pred_df$obs, pred_df$pred)
  list(cm = cm, auc = auc, mae = mae)
}

metrics_multinom <- get_metrics(pred_multinom)
metrics_tree     <- get_metrics(pred_tree)
metrics_knn      <- get_metrics(pred_knn)
metrics_svm      <- get_metrics(pred_svm)
metrics_xgb      <- get_metrics(pred_xgb)

# ============================================================
# Macro-Averaged Metrics Function (Now Includes MAE)
# ============================================================
macro_metrics <- function(metrics) {
  df <- as.data.frame(metrics$cm$byClass)
  data.frame(
    F1 = mean(df$F1, na.rm = TRUE),
    Precision = mean(df$Precision, na.rm = TRUE),
    Recall = mean(df$Recall, na.rm = TRUE),
    AUC = as.numeric(metrics$auc),
    MAE = metrics$mae
  )
}

# ============================================================
# Combine All Results into a Single Table
# ============================================================
results_df <- bind_rows(
  XGBoost              = macro_metrics(metrics_xgb),
  Decision_Tree        = macro_metrics(metrics_tree),
  SVM                  = macro_metrics(metrics_svm),
  Multinomial_Logistic = macro_metrics(metrics_multinom),
  KNN                  = macro_metrics(metrics_knn),
  .id = "Model"
) %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(., 3)))

# ============================================================
# Display Table
# ============================================================
results_df %>%
  kable(
    caption = "Model Results Summary",
    col.names = c("Model", "F1", "Precision", "Recall", "AUC", "MAE"),
    align = "lccccc",
    format = "html"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("condensed"),
    full_width = FALSE,
    position = "center",
    font_size = 12
  ) %>%
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, extra_css = "padding: 4px 8px;") %>%
  row_spec(1:nrow(results_df), extra_css = "padding: 2px 8px; line-height: 1.2;") %>%
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE) %>%
  add_header_above(c(" " = 1, "Macro-Averaged Metrics" = 5))
```

The results demonstrate clear performance differences across models,
with XGBoost achieving the highest F1-score (0.677) and AUC (0.908),
indicating superior predictive accuracy and class separation capability.
The Decision Tree performed comparably (F1 = 0.648, AUC = 0.896), though
its simpler structure resulted in slightly reduced accuracy, while SVM
achieved similar results (F1 = 0.632, AUC = 0.897) but exhibited some
difficulty with overlapping class boundaries. Multinomial Logistic
Regression provided a strong linear baseline (F1 = 0.623, AUC = 0.895),
effectively capturing key relationships while maintaining
interpretability, whereas KNN performed weakest (F1 = 0.507, AUC =
0.843), likely due to challenges with high dimensionality and
sensitivity to feature scaling. Overall, XGBoost proved most effective
at capturing the complex, non-linear patterns influencing female
management representation, justifying its selection as the optimal model
for this classification task.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Confusion Matrix Analysis

```{r fig-confmatrix, fig.cap="Confusion Matrix", message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.width=12, fig.height=6}
# ============================================================
# Confusion Matrix Heatmaps (Normalized by Actual Class)
# ============================================================

library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Combine all confusion matrices
cm_list <- list(
  "Multinomial" = cm_multinom,
  "Tree" = cm_tree,
  "KNN" = cm_knn,
  "SVM" = cm_svm,
  "XGBoost" = cm_xgb
)

# Build a combined and normalized dataframe
cm_df <- bind_rows(lapply(names(cm_list), function(model) {
  df <- as.data.frame(cm_list[[model]]$table)
  names(df) <- c("Predicted", "Actual", "Count")
  df$Model <- model
  df %>%
    group_by(Actual) %>%
    mutate(Percent = Count / sum(Count) * 100)
}))

cm_df <- cm_df %>%
  mutate(
    Model = factor(Model, levels = c("XGBoost", "Tree", "SVM", "Multinomial", "KNN"  )),
    Actual = factor(Actual, levels = c("V.Low", "Low", "Mod", "High", "V.High")),
    Predicted = factor(Predicted, levels = c("V.Low", "Low", "Mod", "High", "V.High"))
  )

# ============================================================
# Plot normalized confusion matrices side-by-side
# ============================================================
ggplot(cm_df, aes(x = Actual, y = Predicted, fill = Percent)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f%%", Percent)), size = 4) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#f7fbff", high = "#08519c") +
  facet_wrap(~ Model, ncol = 3) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 12),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 12),
    axis.title.x = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
    axis.title.y = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 11),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 10)
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Actual Management Representation Level",
    y = "Predicted Level",
    fill = "%"
  )

```

The confusion matrices reveal consistent patterns across all models,
with stronger performance at extreme categories (Very Low and Very High)
and greater overlap in middle categories, reflecting the ordinal nature
of the classification task. XGBoost and Decision Tree models achieved
the clearest diagonals, correctly identifying most organisations in the
Very Low and Very High female management categories, though they
exhibited moderate confusion between adjacent classes such as Low versus
Moderate, which is expected given gradual transitions in workforce
composition. SVM and Multinomial Logistic Regression demonstrated solid
performance with slightly more dispersion across neighboring levels,
indicating reduced precision at class boundaries, while KNN exhibited
substantial misclassification across all categories, particularly
underestimating mid-level organisations. Overall, the confusion matrices
confirm that while all models distinguish extreme cases most
effectively, ensemble and tree-based approaches, especially XGBoost,
capture the underlying structure of management representation with
superior accuracy and consistency.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Training vs Cross Validation Accuracy

```{r}
models <- list(
  "Multinomial" = multinom_cv,
  "Tree" = tree_cv,
  "KNN" = knn_cv,
  "XGBoost" = xgb_cv,
  "SVM" = svm_cv
)

accuracy_comparison <- lapply(models, function(model) {
  train_acc <- mean(predict(model, newdata = modeling_df) == modeling_df$management_representation_level)
  cv_acc <- mean(model$resample$Accuracy)
  data.frame(
    Model = model$method,
    Train = round(train_acc, 3),
    CV = round(cv_acc, 3),
    Gap = round(train_acc - cv_acc, 3)
  )
}) %>% bind_rows()

accuracy_comparison %>%
  kbl(
    caption = "Training vs CV Accuracy",
    col.names = c("Model", "Train Accuracy", "CV Accuracy", "Gap"),
    align = "lccc",
    format = "html"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("condensed"),
    full_width = FALSE,
    position = "center",
    font_size = 12
  ) %>%
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, extra_css = "padding: 4px 8px;") %>%
  row_spec(1:nrow(accuracy_comparison), extra_css = "padding: 2px 8px; line-height: 1.2;") %>%
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE)
```

The training versus cross-validation accuracy comparison reveals varying
degrees of model generalisation across classifiers, with Multinomial
Logistic Regression demonstrating the smallest performance gap (0.025),
indicating stable generalisation and minimal overfitting consistent with
its linear and regularised structure. Decision Tree and k-Nearest
Neighbours models also display relatively small gaps (0.036 and 0.038
respectively), suggesting moderate complexity and balanced bias-variance
trade-offs, while SVM and XGBoost achieve the highest training
accuracies but exhibit noticeably larger gaps (0.135 and 0.124
respectively), reflecting overfitting to the training data. This pattern
is attributable to the greater model flexibility of SVM and XGBoost,
combined with practical constraints of limited hyperparameter tuning due
to extended training times of several hours. Consequently, while these
non-linear models effectively capture complex feature interactions,
their generalisation performance suffers compared to simpler and faster
models which maintains more consistent accuracy across training and
validation folds.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Feature importance

For conciseness, this section focuses on the Multinomial Logistic
Regression model, which offers clear coefficient-based insights into
feature effects. The feature importance results for the tree-based
models (Decision Tree and XGBoost) are presented in the Appendix for
reference.

[Table 4: Top 3 Overall Features (Left) vs Top 3 Policy Features (Right)
by
Class]{style="color: #6e6e6e; font-size: 115%; font-family:Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif;"}

```{r top_features_combined, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.width=15, fig.height=6}
# ============================================================
# Combined Display: Top Overall Features + Top Policy Features
# ============================================================
library(dplyr)
library(broom)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(htmltools)

# ---------- Common setup ----------
coef_all <- tidy(multinom_cv$finalModel) %>%
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") %>%
  rename(Feature = term, Class = y.level, Coefficient = estimate, StdError = std.error) %>%
  mutate(
    z_value = abs(Coefficient / StdError),
    Stars = case_when(
      z_value > 2.58 ~ "***",   # p < 0.01
      z_value > 1.96 ~ "**",    # p < 0.05
      z_value > 1.64 ~ "*",     # p < 0.10
      TRUE ~ ""
    ),
    Formatted = sprintf("%.3f%s", Coefficient, Stars),
    Class = factor(Class, levels = c("Low", "Mod", "High", "V.High"))
  )

# ---------- Top 3 overall features per class ----------
top_by_class <- coef_all %>%
  group_by(Class) %>%
  arrange(desc(abs(Coefficient)), .by_group = TRUE) %>%
  slice_head(n = 3) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(Class, Feature, Formatted)

table1 <- kbl(
  top_by_class,
  align = "lcc",
  col.names = c("Class", "Top Overall Feature", "Coefficient")
) %>%
  kable_styling(
    full_width = FALSE,
    bootstrap_options = c("condensed"),
    font_size = 12,
    position = "left"
  ) %>%
  collapse_rows(columns = 1, valign = "middle") %>%
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, extra_css = "padding: 4px 8px;") %>%
  row_spec(1:nrow(top_by_class), extra_css = "padding: 2px 8px; line-height: 1.2;")

# ---------- Top 3 policy (binary) features per class ----------
binary_vars <- c(
  "has_gender_equality_strategy",
  "has_target_for_gender_equity",
  "has_policy_for_gender_equality",
  "has_target_for_women_in_governing_body",
  "has_pay_equity_strategy",
  "did_pay_gap_analysis",
  "actioned_on_pay_gap_analysis",
  "consulted_employee_on_equity",
  "has_flexible_work_policy",
  "offers_working_from_home",
  "offers_paid_primary_carer_leave",
  "offers_paid_secondary_carer_leave",
  "opt_out_approach_for_parental_leave",
  "has_strategy_for_employee_with_family",
  "has_target_for_men_parental_leave",
  "gender_equality_training_for_managers"
)

top_bin_by_class <- coef_all %>%
  filter(Feature %in% binary_vars) %>%
  group_by(Class) %>%
  arrange(desc(abs(Coefficient)), .by_group = TRUE) %>%
  slice_head(n = 3) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(Class, Feature, Formatted)

table2 <- kbl(
  top_bin_by_class,
  align = "lcc",
  col.names = c("Class", "Top Policy Feature", "Coefficient")
) %>%
  kable_styling(
    full_width = FALSE,
    bootstrap_options = c("condensed"),
    font_size = 12,
    position = "left"
  ) %>%
  collapse_rows(columns = 1, valign = "middle") %>%
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, extra_css = "padding: 4px 8px;") %>%
  row_spec(1:nrow(top_bin_by_class), extra_css = "padding: 2px 8px; line-height: 1.2;") 

# ---------- Combine and render properly ----------
HTML(paste0(
  '<div style="display: flex; justify-content: space-between; gap: 2%; align-items: flex-start;">',
  '<div style="width: 48%;">', table1, '</div>',
  '<div style="width: 48%;">', table2, '</div>',
  '</div>'
))

```

The MLR results reveal that workforce composition serves as the
strongest predictor of female management representation, with the
proportion of full-time female employees and female executives showing
substantial positive effects that increase progressively from Low to
Very High categories. The Health Care and Social Assistance industry
consistently displays negative coefficients despite high overall female
participation, suggesting persistent structural barriers to management
advancement, while the negative effect of overall full-time employment
at higher management levels implies that industries offering greater
workplace flexibility may better support women's progression into
leadership positions.

When controlling for workforce composition, flexible work policies
emerge as the most consistent and statistically significant
policy-related enabler across management levels, alongside positive
effects from targets for women in governing bodies and gender equality
training for managers. In contrast, pay equity strategies and pay gap
analysis display small negative coefficients, likely reflecting reactive
adoption by organisations still addressing underlying structural
imbalances. Overall, while workforce composition remains the dominant
factor, proactive organisational policies promoting flexibility and
leadership accountability serve as important complementary enablers of
gender equity in leadership roles.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Conclusion and Future Work

### Summary

This analysis demonstrates the effectiveness of applying diverse machine
learning algorithms to a high-dimensional multi-class classification
problem. Among the five models evaluated (XGBoost, Decision Tree, SVM,
Multinomial Logistic Regression, and KNN) XGBoost achieved the best
performance with a macro F1-score of 0.677 and AUC of 0.908,
demonstrating the advantages of ensemble methods in capturing complex
non-linear relationships, though at the cost of longer training times
and greater overfitting compared to simpler models. Decision Trees and
SVM also performed well (F1 ≈ 0.65 and 0.63 respectively), while KNN
struggled with the high-dimensional feature space. Feature importance
analysis consistently identified workforce composition variables,
particularly the proportion of women in full-time and executive
positions, as the dominant predictors across all models, while binary
policy indicators contributed meaningful but substantially weaker
predictive signals.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Limitations and Future Work

Several important limitations constrain the interpretation of these
findings. The cross-sectional nature of the data prevents causal claims
about what drives changes in female management representation, allowing
only identification of associations between organisational
characteristics and current representation levels. Self-reporting by
organisations may introduce systematic biases, while the decision to
categorise organisations into five quintile-based groups may obscure
important within-category variation and create artificial boundaries
between similar organisations.

Future research should address these limitations through longitudinal
analysis using multiple years of WGEA data, enabling examination of
whether changes in workforce composition or policy adoption lead to
subsequent improvements in management representation. Methodologically,
treating the target variable as continuous rather than categorical could
simplify the modeling approach and potentially improve predictive
accuracy, while incorporating additional contextual variables such as
organisational culture surveys and geographical economic conditions
could provide richer understanding of factors shaping gender equity
outcomes.

## Appendix

### Raw Data File Summary

The table below summarises these seven source files, including their
dimensions and the number of numerical and categorical features within
each dataset. The high row counts seen across all files are a direct
result of this granular, one-to-many structure (one employer to many
rows).

```{r compact-dataset-summary, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(kableExtra)

# Create the data frame with the new combined 'Details' column
datasets <- data.frame(
  Name = c(
    "Workforce Composition",
    "Workforce Management Statistics",
    "Harm Prevention",
    "Action on Gender Equality",
    "Flexible Work",
    "Workplace Overview",
    "Employee Support"
  ),
  Details = c(
    "193916 × 17<br>(2 Num, 15 Cat)",
    "186831 × 18<br>(2 Num, 16 Cat)",
    "508476 × 20<br>(1 Num, 19 Cat)",
    "129313 × 20<br>(1 Num, 19 Cat)",
    "222858 × 20<br>(1 Num, 19 Cat)",
    "178111 × 20<br>(1 Num, 19 Cat)",
    "289830 × 20<br>(1 Num, 19 Cat)"
  ),
  Description = c(
    "Detailed employee counts by occupation, manager category, employment status, type, and gender, giving a breakdown of workforce structure across employers and industries.",
    "Quantitative statistics related to workforce movements (e.g., promotions, resignations, leave uptake) for each organisation, disaggregated by gender, employment status, and managerial category.",
    "Questionnaire on policies and initiatives aimed at preventing workplace harm.",
    "Questionaire on emploter's actions, policies, and initiatives related to promoting gender equality.",
    "Questionnaire on the employer's flexible work policies and practices.",
    "Questionnaire on key organisational attributes and responses to broad workplace-related employer practices and structures.",
    "Questionnaire on measures organisations have in place to support employees wellbeing."
  ),
  check.names = FALSE,
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

datasets %>%
  kbl(
    col.names = c(
      "Name",
      "Dimensions<br>(Rows×Cols)",
      "Description"
    ),
    escape = FALSE,
    booktabs = TRUE
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("condensed"),
    full_width = TRUE,
    font_size = 12,
    position = "center",
    latex_options = "scale_down"
  ) %>%
  column_spec(1, width = "7em") %>%
  column_spec(2, width = "9em") %>%
  column_spec(3, width = "35em") %>%
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, extra_css = "padding: 4px 8px;") %>%
  row_spec(1:nrow(datasets), extra_css = "padding: 2px 8px; line-height: 1.2;") %>%
  row_spec(0, extra_latex_after = "\\renewcommand{\\arraystretch}{0.9}")
```

### Top Features - Decision Tree

```{r fig-DecisionTreeFeature, fig.cap="Top Decision Tree Features", fig.height=4, fig.width=6}
# ============================================================
# DECISION TREE FEATURE IMPORTANCE
# ============================================================
binary_vars <- c(
  "has_gender_equality_strategy",
  "has_target_for_gender_equity",
  "has_policy_for_gender_equality",
  "has_target_for_women_in_governing_body",
  "has_pay_equity_strategy",
  "did_pay_gap_analysis",
  "actioned_on_pay_gap_analysis",
  "consulted_employee_on_equity",
  "has_flexible_work_policy",
  "offers_working_from_home",
  "offers_paid_primary_carer_leave",
  "offers_paid_secondary_carer_leave",
  "opt_out_approach_for_parental_leave",
  "has_strategy_for_employee_with_family",
  "has_target_for_men_parental_leave",
  "gender_equality_training_for_managers"
)

dt_imp <- varImp(tree_cv)$importance %>%
  rownames_to_column("Feature") %>%
  arrange(desc(Overall))

# --- Top 5 overall ---
dt_top5 <- dt_imp %>% slice_head(n = 5)

# --- Top 5 policy-related features ---
dt_policy <- dt_imp %>%
  filter(Feature %in% binary_vars) %>%
  slice_head(n = 5)

# --- Plot: Top 5 overall ---
p_dt_top5 <- ggplot(dt_top5, aes(x = reorder(Feature, Overall), y = Overall)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#3182bd") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Top 5 Most Important Features (Decision Tree)",
    x = "Feature",
    y = "Importance"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

# --- Plot: Top 5 policy features ---
p_dt_policy <- ggplot(dt_policy, aes(x = reorder(Feature, Overall), y = Overall)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#6baed6") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Top 5 Policy Features (Decision Tree)",
    x = "Policy Feature",
    y = "Importance"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

# --- Combine DT plots vertically ---
(p_dt_top5 / p_dt_policy) +
  plot_annotation(title = "Decision Tree Feature Importance") &
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5))
```

*Note: The limited number of policy features in the figure reflects the
Decision Tree’s complexity pruning, which retained only two
policy-related variables after model optimisation.*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Top Features - XGBoost

```{r fig-XGBoostFeatures, fig.cap="Top XGBoost Features", fig.height=4, fig.width=6}
# ============================================================
# XGBOOST FEATURE IMPORTANCE
# ============================================================

xgb_imp <- varImp(xgb_cv)$importance %>%
  rownames_to_column("Feature") %>%
  arrange(desc(Overall))

# --- Top 5 overall ---
xgb_top5 <- xgb_imp %>% slice_head(n = 5)

# --- Top 5 policy-related features ---
xgb_policy <- xgb_imp %>%
  filter(grepl("has_|offers_|did_|actioned_|gender_|target_", Feature)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 5)

# --- Plot: Top 5 overall ---
p_xgb_top5 <- ggplot(xgb_top5, aes(x = reorder(Feature, Overall), y = Overall)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#e6550d") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Top 5 Most Important Features (XGBoost)",
    x = "Feature",
    y = "Importance"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

# --- Plot: Top 5 policy features ---
p_xgb_policy <- ggplot(xgb_policy, aes(x = reorder(Feature, Overall), y = Overall)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#fd8d3c") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Top 5 Policy Features (XGBoost)",
    x = "Policy Feature",
    y = "Importance"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

# --- Combine XGB plots vertically ---
(p_xgb_top5 / p_xgb_policy) +
  plot_annotation(title = "XGBoost Feature Importance") &
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5))

```

## References

[1] Workplace Gender Equality Agency and Bankwest Curtin Economics
Centre, Gender Equity Insights 2020: Delivering on Business Outcomes.
Perth, WA: WGEA and BCEC, 2020. [Online]. Available:
<https://www.wgea.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/BCEC%20WGEA%20Media%20Release%20-%20More%20women%20at%20the%20top%20proves%20better%20for%20business.pdf>
[Accessed: Oct. 30, 2025]

[2] Workplace Gender Equality Agency, "WGEA Public Dataset 2024,"
*data.gov.au*, Jul. 30, 2025. [Online]. Available:
<https://data.gov.au/data/dataset/wgea-dataset/resource/f12cc138-44a8-45fc-9ba7-97ee5dadd683>.
[Accessed: Sep. 21, 2025].

## Generative-AI Declaration

Generative AI was used in the preparation of this report. It helped
generate R code based on our specified analysis plan and helped draft
paragraphs from our prompts. All core intellectual contributions, from
the initial strategy to the final conclusions, are our own, and all
AI-generated text was critically reviewed and edited by the authors.
